pages.raw_text:
In Washington d. e., there police court Judge who
regards sleep crime His name is |. G. Kimba. One
day last week this judge imposed fine of $40 on coun
tryman whom Policeman Schultz found asleep in his
wagon.


The horse Flooding patiently along in the vicinity
of Washington circle having covered the 20 slow miles
from the little truck farm in Maryland The horse knew
the way, having travelled the road many times before It
stand at Center Market He made his living selling the
vegetables raised the few scrubby which he rent
ed, and the chickens which his wife and children helped
raise He did not get rich at it; but he managed to get
along


But it is long drive over MARYLAND roads, and the
business at Center Market begins before breakfast So the
counryman who expects to find purchaser for his eggs
chickens and vegetables must start early early fact
that it is the night before This had started at 1
o'clock The previous day he had worked at hayng and
with the cutvator from to 6. So he both tired
and sleepy And jogging the long road in the early
morning dawn the countryman went to sleep.


The policeman reported finding the asleep, and the
wagon drawn along Washington circle by the patient
intelligence of the unguided horse.


''Ten dollars and COSTS said Judge Kimball 'Ten
dollars equivalent term in the workhouse."


This is the Judge l. g. Kmball who imposed fine
of $500 Robert Harper president Of the Washington
chamber of for violating the pure food law in
the sale of deadly opiate "brain food" and head
ache s'cure' The governments attorney asked that
Jail sentence be imposed in this He pointed out the
obvious fact that fine of $500 would be punishment
OF great wealth like Harper and urged that the
penalties of law be made to bear with equal weight the
rich and poor


But Judge G. Kmball is not that kind of judge. The
president of Washngtons chamber of commerce. seller of
thousands of bottles of poisoned stuff to unsuspectng
vads this puveyor OF pale DEATH the incidental director
OF prosperous bank, let off with the payment of
nominal fine-what to him small change.


But the tired countryman, falling asleep in his wagon,
is fined the really, to him, large Of So


On, law, what injustces committed in thy name!
